questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, remember that PLESAs are designed for non-highly compensated employees and are funded with after-tax contributions, making them distinct from traditional employer-sponsored retirement plans.
questions.memoryAid: PLEAS: Penalty-free, Linked, Emergency, After-tax, Savings (up to $2,500).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the features of pension-linked emergency savings accounts (PLESAs) with traditional retirement accounts. A common trap is assuming vesting schedules apply, similar to employer matching contributions in standard 401(k) plans. PLESAs are designed for immediate access, so vesting is not applicable. Also, test-takers often incorrectly assume that PLESAs are only for highly compensated employees, when in fact, they are designed to help lower and middle-income workers build emergency savings.

questions.question: A non-highly compensated employee wants to build an emergency fund through their 401(k). Under SECURE 2.0\'s emergency savings account provision:
questions.explanation: PLESA features: Separate from 401(k) deferral (doesn\'t reduce limit). Max $2,500 balance—excess auto-transfers to 401(k). Non-HCE only. Must allow monthly withdrawals minimum. First 4/year no penalty, after that same as regular distribution. Employer may auto-enroll at up to 3%. Goal: emergency access without raiding retirement.
options: Funds can only be accessed after age 59½, unless the employee experiences a qualifying event such as hardship, disability, or separation from service, subject to plan rules.

The employer is required to match emergency account contributions up to a certain percentage of the employee's salary, similar to the standard 401(k) match, but only for employees earning under $50,000 annually.
The account balance is automatically transferred to the 401(k) when it exceeds $2,500, and the employee may withdraw funds at least monthly without penalty
Contributions reduce their 401(k) deferral limit by the amount contributed to the emergency savings account, and these contributions are treated as after-tax, impacting overall retirement savings.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Emergency savings accounts under SECURE 2.0 allow penalty-free withdrawals at least monthly, regardless of age or qualifying event. The age 59 1/2 rule applies to regular 401(k) withdrawals.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Employer matching is not required for emergency savings accounts; it's optional for the employer to provide. The $50,000 income limit is also incorrect; matching is not tied to income.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - This accurately describes key features of a PLESA under SECURE 2.0. The account balance is capped at $2,500 (indexed for inflation), and amounts exceeding this limit are automatically transferred to the employee's 401(k). Furthermore, employees must be allowed to withdraw funds at least monthly without penalty for the first four withdrawals each year.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Contributions to the emergency savings account do *not* reduce the 401(k) deferral limit. They are generally made with after-tax dollars, but do not impact the overall retirement savings limit.

questions.educational: The SECURE 2.0 Act introduced Pension-Linked Emergency Savings Accounts (PLESAs) to help non-highly compensated employees build emergency savings within their retirement plans. This provision addresses the common issue of individuals raiding their retirement accounts for unexpected expenses. By providing a readily accessible, penalty-free emergency fund, the SECURE 2.0 Act aims to improve retirement security by reducing the need for early withdrawals from traditional retirement savings.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, remember that PLESAs are specifically for non-highly compensated employees and that the $2,500 limit is a key feature to differentiate it from other retirement savings options. Also, focus on the withdrawal rules and the lack of mandatory employer matching.
questions.memoryAid: PLESA: 'Protecting Lives with Emergency Savings Access' – emphasizes the purpose and accessibility.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the accessibility of funds in a Pension-Linked Emergency Savings Account (PLESA) with standard 401(k) restrictions. A common trap is assuming funds are locked until retirement age. Also, test-takers often incorrectly believe employers are *required* to match contributions to these accounts. While employers *may* choose to match, it is not mandated by SECURE 2.0. Finally, some incorrectly assume contributions reduce the 401(k) deferral limit, when they are a separate feature.

questions.question: Under SECURE 2.0, the catch-up contribution limit for 401(k) plans for participants ages 60-63 beginning in 2025 will be:
questions.explanation: SECURE 2.0 "super catch-up" (effective 2025): Ages 60-63 can contribute the greater of $10,000 OR 150% of the regular catch-up ($7,500 × 150% = $11,250). This $10,000/$11,250 limit will be indexed for inflation starting 2026. Example: 2025 regular limit $23,500 + $11,250 = $34,750 max for ages 60-63. Returns to standard catch-up at 64. Helps late savers accelerate.
options: Only available for Roth contributions if the participant's prior year wages exceeded $145,000, otherwise disallowed.
The greater of $10,000 or 150% of the regular catch-up limit (indexed for inflation after 2025)
Eliminated entirely for this age group if their employer sponsors a defined benefit plan, to encourage focus on that plan.
$7,500, the same as participants age 50-59, but only if the participant is considered a highly compensated employee.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While SECURE 2.0 does require catch-up contributions to be Roth for those earning over a certain amount, it doesn't disallow them entirely. The increased catch-up limit applies regardless of Roth status.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - SECURE 2.0 introduces a "super catch-up" contribution for individuals ages 60-63, starting in 2025. This provision allows them to contribute the greater of $10,000 or 150% of the regular catch-up limit, providing a larger opportunity to save for retirement. The limit will be indexed for inflation beginning in 2026.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The legislation does not eliminate catch-up contributions for those with defined benefit plans. The catch-up provision is independent of defined benefit plan participation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The $7,500 limit applies to those aged 50-59 in 2023 (indexed for inflation in future years). The 60-63 age group has a different, higher limit under SECURE 2.0, and it's not tied to HCE status.

questions.educational: The SECURE 2.0 Act aims to improve retirement security by increasing contribution limits and expanding access to retirement plans. The "super catch-up" provision for ages 60-63 is designed to help those who may have started saving later in life to accelerate their retirement savings during their peak earning years. This aligns with the CFP Board's focus on helping clients achieve their retirement goals through strategic planning and maximizing available savings opportunities.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to the age ranges specified in questions about retirement plan contributions, as different age groups may have different contribution limits and rules under SECURE 2.0.
questions.memoryAid: 60-63 = Super Catch-Up (SECURE 2.0)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the specific changes to catch-up contribution limits introduced by SECURE 2.0. While SECURE 2.0 does include provisions related to Roth contributions, it doesn't *only* apply to them. A common trap is assuming the catch-up limit remains the same across all ages 50+, or that it is eliminated entirely. The exam exploits the misconception that the catch-up rules are static.

questions.question: Sarah, age 61, participates in her employer's SIMPLE IRA. Under SECURE 2.0, which SIMPLE IRA participant benefits from the enhanced catch-up provision?
questions.explanation: SIMPLE IRA enhanced catch-up (ages 60-63): Greater of $5,000 or 150% of regular catch-up ($3,500 × 150% = $5,250). Regular catch-up (50+): $3,500. So ages 60-63 can contribute extra $5,250 vs $3,500. Combined 2025: $16,000 deferral + $5,250 catch-up = $21,250. At 64, reverts to $3,500. Similar 150% boost for SIMPLE, SEP catch-ups.
options: A 45-year-old high earner whose compensation exceeds $145,000, seeking Roth contributions.
A 61-year-old who can contribute an additional $5,000 (or 150% of normal catch-up)
A 65-year-old business owner who wants to make traditional catch-up contributions.
A 55-year-old maximizing contributions and expecting to take distributions before age 59 1/2.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The enhanced catch-up provision under SECURE 2.0 applies to individuals aged 60-63, not based on income or Roth contributions. The income threshold impacts regular catch-up contributions, not the enhanced catch-up.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The SECURE 2.0 Act introduced an enhanced catch-up contribution for SIMPLE IRA participants aged 60-63. This allows them to contribute the greater of $5,000 or 150% of the regular catch-up amount. In this case, 150% of the regular catch-up ($3,500) is $5,250, so the individual can contribute an additional $5,250, making this the correct answer.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a 65-year-old can make regular catch-up contributions, the enhanced catch-up provision is specifically for those aged 60-63. This option refers to the standard catch-up, not the SECURE 2.0 enhanced catch-up.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The enhanced catch-up provision under SECURE 2.0 applies to individuals aged 60-63, not based on contribution levels or distribution timing. Early distributions are a separate issue and not related to the enhanced catch-up provision.

questions.educational: The SECURE 2.0 Act aimed to improve retirement savings opportunities, particularly for those nearing retirement. The enhanced catch-up provisions for SIMPLE IRAs (and other plans) between ages 60 and 63 are designed to allow individuals to boost their savings during these critical years before retirement. This aligns with the CFP Board's focus on retirement income planning and maximizing retirement savings.
questions.examTip: Remember the specific age range (60-63) for the SECURE 2.0 enhanced catch-up provision on the CFP exam. Pay close attention to age details in retirement planning questions.
questions.memoryAid: SECURE 2.0: '60 to 63, boost for me!'
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are confused about the age requirements for the enhanced catch-up provisions under SECURE 2.0. The SECURE 2.0 enhanced catch-up applies to those ages 60-63. A common trap is assuming the enhanced catch-up applies to all participants over 50, or that it is based on income level. The exam exploits the misconception that catch-up contributions are uniform across all retirement plans and ages.

questions.question: A 35-year-old employee in the 24% tax bracket receives a $6,000 employer 401(k) match. If she elects the Roth option for employer contributions under SECURE 2.0:
questions.explanation: Choosing Roth employer match: $6,000 × 24% = $1,440 current tax. BUT: 30 years of tax-free growth. If invested at 7%: $6,000 → ~$45,676 tax-free. Break-even: if future tax rate stays same, Roth wins due to tax-free earnings growth. Employer keeps deduction. Match immediately 100% vested when designated Roth. Trade-off: current tax for future tax-free growth.
options: She receives no immediate benefit, as employer Roth matches are taxed upon distribution, negating upfront Roth advantages.
The employer loses their tax deduction on the matched amount, and the employee's taxable income is reduced accordingly.
The match must be withdrawn within 5 years or face a 10% penalty, severely limiting its long-term retirement benefits.
She will owe $1,440 in current income tax but gain tax-free growth for 30 years

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Employer Roth matches are taxed *now*, not upon distribution. The benefit is tax-free growth, not avoiding taxes altogether.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Employers *do* get a tax deduction for Roth contributions, just like traditional 401(k) contributions. The employee pays the tax now.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The 5-year rule applies to Roth IRA *contributions*, not employer Roth 401(k) *matches*. The match is subject to normal 401(k) distribution rules.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Electing Roth treatment for the $6,000 employer match means the employee will pay income tax on the match in the current year. At a 24% tax bracket, this equates to $1,440 ($6,000 x 24%). The benefit is that all future growth and qualified withdrawals from this Roth account will be tax-free, potentially over a 30-year investment horizon.

questions.educational: SECURE 2.0 introduced the option for employees to designate employer matching contributions as Roth, offering a trade-off between paying taxes now versus potentially avoiding taxes on future growth and withdrawals. This decision hinges on factors like current vs. expected future tax rates and the time horizon until retirement. Understanding Roth vs. traditional tax treatment is crucial for retirement planning, impacting both accumulation and distribution strategies.
questions.examTip: When evaluating Roth vs. traditional retirement plan contributions on the CFP exam, always consider the client's current and expected future tax bracket, as well as their time horizon until retirement. A longer time horizon generally favors Roth due to the potential for significant tax-free growth.
questions.memoryAid: Roth Now, Grow Later: Remember that Roth contributions mean paying taxes now for tax-free growth later.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware that SECURE 2.0 allows for Roth employer contributions. A common trap is assuming that employer matches cannot be Roth. Also, test-takers often incorrectly believe the employer loses their tax deduction when making Roth contributions. The exam exploits the misconception that employer contributions are always pre-tax.

questions.question: GreenTech Solutions is exploring SECURE 2.0's student loan matching provision. Under this provision, employers may:
questions.explanation: SECURE 2.0 student loan matching (effective 2024): Employer can treat qualified student loan payments as elective deferrals for matching purposes. Employee doesn\'t need to contribute to 401(k) to get match. Example: Employee pays $500/month to loans, employer matches as if $500 went to 401(k). Self-certification allowed. Addresses: young workers can\'t save due to loans. Employers get same deduction as regular match.
options: Deduct student loan payments from employee paychecks and apply them to 401(k)
Make matching contributions to an employee\
t contribute to the plan
Pay off student loans directly as a tax-free benefit

whyWrong.A: Why option A is WRONG - While employers can facilitate student loan payments through payroll deductions, SECURE 2.0's matching provision focuses on treating these payments as elective deferrals for the *purpose* of calculating employer matching contributions, not directly applying the deductions to the 401(k).
whyWrong.C: Why option C is WRONG - SECURE 2.0 *permits* employers to contribute to the plan based on student loan payments; it doesn't mandate it. Therefore, stating they 'can't contribute' is incorrect, as the provision provides an *option* for employers.
whyWrong.D: Why option D is WRONG - SECURE 2.0 does not allow employers to directly pay off student loans as a tax-free benefit. The provision focuses on *matching contributions* to retirement plans based on student loan payments, not direct loan repayment assistance.

questions.educational: SECURE 2.0's student loan matching provision aims to address the conflict many young professionals face between paying down student debt and saving for retirement. By allowing employers to treat student loan payments as 401(k) contributions for matching purposes, it incentivizes retirement savings even while individuals are burdened with debt. This provision aligns with the broader goal of increasing retirement security, a key focus of the CFP exam.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to the wording of questions related to SECURE 2.0. Distinguish between direct loan repayment assistance (not part of the provision) and matching contributions based on loan payments (the core of the provision).
questions.memoryAid: Think 'SLM': Student Loan Matching allows employers to match based on loan payments.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unfamiliar with SECURE 2.0's student loan matching provision. While employers can facilitate student loan payments, they don't directly apply them to 401(k)s. A common trap is assuming employers can directly pay off student loans as a tax-free benefit. SECURE 2.0 *permits* employers to make matching contributions based on student loan payments, but it's not mandatory.

questions.question: SECURE 2.0 requires automatic enrollment for new 401(k) and 403(b) plans established after December 29, 2022. The initial automatic enrollment rate must be:
questions.explanation: SECURE 2.0 auto-enrollment mandate (effective 2025 for new plans): 3-10% initial rate. Auto-escalation: 1%/year up to at least 10%, max 15%. Exemptions: small businesses (<10 employees), new businesses (<3 years), church plans, governmental plans. Existing plans grandfathered. Employee can opt out. QDIA rules apply. Dramatically increases participation vs. voluntary enrollment.
options: At least 3% but not more than 10%, with annual auto-escalation of 1% until reaching at least 10% (up to 15%)
Determined entirely by employee election, unless the employer opts to provide a default enrollment rate of 5% with no auto-escalation.
At least 15% to match typical savings rates recommended by financial advisors, with no auto-escalation required under the new regulations.
Exactly 6% of compensation, with no auto-escalation permitted, as a simplified approach to encourage broader participation in retirement savings.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - SECURE 2.0 mandates that new 401(k) and 403(b) plans established after December 29, 2022, must automatically enroll eligible employees at a rate of at least 3% but no more than 10% of compensation. The plan must also include automatic escalation of 1% per year until the employee contribution rate reaches at least 10% (but no more than 15%).
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - SECURE 2.0 mandates automatic enrollment with a minimum rate. While employers can offer higher rates or other features, they cannot eliminate the automatic enrollment component entirely.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While 15% is a good savings target, SECURE 2.0 sets a lower minimum initial enrollment rate and requires auto-escalation. It does not mandate a 15% starting point.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - SECURE 2.0 requires a minimum of 3% and allows for auto-escalation. A fixed 6% is not compliant with the auto-escalation requirements and doesn't meet the minimum starting point in some cases.

questions.educational: SECURE 2.0 aims to improve retirement savings outcomes by addressing common behavioral barriers to participation, such as inertia. Automatic enrollment leverages the 'default effect,' where individuals tend to stick with the default option, leading to higher participation rates and increased retirement savings. Understanding the specific provisions of SECURE 2.0 is crucial for advising clients on plan design and maximizing retirement savings opportunities, a key component of the Retirement Savings and Income Planning domain.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to the effective dates and specific percentage thresholds associated with SECURE 2.0 provisions, as these are frequently tested.
questions.memoryAid: Think '3-10-15': 3% minimum initial enrollment, up to 10% initial enrollment, and up to 15% maximum with auto-escalation.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the specific automatic enrollment requirements mandated by SECURE 2.0. SECURE 2.0 mandates that new 401(k) and 403(b) plans automatically enroll employees at a rate between 3% and 10%. A common trap is assuming the enrollment rate is determined solely by the employee, or that it must be a higher percentage like 15%. The exam exploits the misconception that automatic enrollment is optional or has different rate parameters.

questions.question: Due to unexpected medical bills, David needs access to funds from his 401(k). Under SECURE 2.0, penalty-free emergency withdrawals from retirement accounts allow:
questions.explanation: SECURE 2.0 emergency expense withdrawal (effective 2024): One per calendar year. Up to $1,000. Self-certification of emergency expense. No 10% penalty. Still subject to income tax. Can repay within 3 years (no new withdrawal until repaid or 3 years pass). No employer approval needed. Aims to reduce hardship withdrawals which have more restrictions.
options: Penalty-free access only for federally declared disasters, up to $25,000 or 50% of the account balance, whichever is less, with no repayment option.
Immediate access to the entire retirement balance, subject to a mandatory 10% early withdrawal penalty and income taxes, regardless of the reason for the withdrawal.
One distribution per year up to $1,000 for emergency personal expense, with option to repay within 3 years
Unlimited withdrawals for any personal expense, but each withdrawal is subject to a 10% penalty, plus applicable federal and state income taxes, without any repayment provisions.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - SECURE 2.0 does allow for emergency withdrawals, but it is not limited to federally declared disasters and has a specific dollar limit and repayment option. Disaster relief withdrawals have different rules.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While you can access your entire retirement balance, it's not penalty-free under the emergency withdrawal provisions of SECURE 2.0. The emergency withdrawal provision allows for a specific amount with an option to repay.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - SECURE 2.0 allows one distribution per year up to $1,000 for emergency personal expenses, with the option to repay the amount within three years. This provision aims to provide accessible funds for unforeseen needs without incurring the standard 10% early withdrawal penalty.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - SECURE 2.0 does not allow for unlimited withdrawals. It provides a limited emergency withdrawal option with a specific dollar amount and repayment provision, not unlimited access.

questions.educational: The SECURE 2.0 Act aims to improve retirement security by addressing financial emergencies without severely impacting long-term savings. This provision balances the need for immediate access to funds with the importance of preserving retirement assets. Understanding the nuances of SECURE 2.0 is crucial for advising clients on navigating unexpected financial hardships while maintaining their retirement plan.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to the specific dollar amounts and conditions associated with SECURE 2.0 provisions, as questions often test your knowledge of these details.
questions.memoryAid: SECURE 2.0: 'One Grand' emergency withdrawal, repay in 'Three Years'.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the specific conditions and limitations surrounding SECURE 2.0's emergency withdrawal provisions. SECURE 2.0's emergency withdrawal provision allows for a limited withdrawal of up to $1,000. A common trap is assuming penalty-free access is only for federally declared disasters, or that it allows for immediate access to the entire retirement balance. The exam exploits the misconception that emergency withdrawals are either unrestricted or only available in extreme circumstances.

questions.question: Emily is a victim of domestic abuse and needs access to retirement funds. SECURE 2.0 adds a penalty exception for domestic abuse victims. This exception allows:
questions.explanation: Domestic abuse victim exception (effective 2024): Self-certification within 1 year of abuse. Up to $10,000 (indexed) or 50% of vested balance, whichever is less. No 10% penalty. Tax can be spread over 3 years. Repayment within 3 years = refund of taxes. No court documentation required. Another SECURE 2.0 provision: financial abuse is recognized abuse type.
options: Withdrawal only if a protective order exists and is verified by the plan administrator, limited to documented expenses related to relocation and medical care.
Distribution directly to a domestic violence shelter, capped at $5,000 annually, and only if the victim resides at the shelter for a minimum of 30 days.
Penalty-free withdrawal up to $10,000 (indexed) or 50% of vested balance, whichever is less, with 3-year repayment option and tax spread over 3 years
Unlimited tax-free withdrawals are permitted, but only from Roth accounts established at least five years prior to the distribution, and subject to a 10% excise tax.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While a protective order can be used as documentation, it is not the only acceptable form, and the withdrawal is not limited to relocation/medical expenses. The actual rule specifies self-certification.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The distribution is not required to go directly to a shelter, and there is no residency requirement. The funds are available to the victim directly.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The SECURE 2.0 Act of 2022 allows victims of domestic abuse to withdraw the *lesser* of $10,000 (indexed for inflation) or 50% of their vested retirement account balance without incurring the 10% early withdrawal penalty. They also have the option to spread the tax liability over three years and can repay the withdrawn amount within three years to receive a refund of the taxes paid.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The domestic abuse exception has a limit and is not tax-free. While Roth accounts have some tax advantages, this exception does not allow unlimited tax-free withdrawals, and the 10% excise tax is incorrect.

questions.educational: The SECURE 2.0 Act aims to improve retirement security by addressing various life events that may necessitate early withdrawals. By providing penalty-free access to retirement funds for domestic abuse victims, the law acknowledges the immediate financial needs that can arise in such situations. This provision aligns with the broader goal of ensuring retirement plans serve as a safety net while encouraging long-term savings.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to the specific dollar limits and conditions associated with the SECURE Act provisions, such as the domestic abuse withdrawal exception. Remember the $10,000 (indexed) or 50% limit and the 3-year repayment/tax spread option.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'SAFE': **S**ECURE Act, **A**buse exception, **F**inancial relief, **E**arly withdrawal (penalty waived).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unfamiliar with the specific details of the domestic abuse victim exception under SECURE 2.0. While a protective order *may* be part of the documentation, it's not the only qualifying factor. A common trap is assuming the distribution must go directly to a shelter, or that withdrawals are unlimited and tax-free. The exam exploits the misconception that the domestic abuse exception is either overly restrictive or completely unrestricted.

questions.question: Maria, born in 1965, is planning her retirement with her advisor at Oak Financial. SECURE 2.0 changed the RMD age. Under SECURE 2.0's RMD changes, at what age must Maria begin taking RMDs?
questions.explanation: SECURE 2.0 RMD ages: Born 1951-1959: Age 73. Born 1960+: Age 75 (effective 2033). Individual born 1965 reaches 75 in 2040, so RMDs begin in 2040. Note: SECURE Act 1.0 raised to 72, SECURE 2.0 raised further. Roth 401(k) RMDs eliminated starting 2024. Traditional IRA/401(k) still have RMDs. Planning: longer deferral = more growth, but larger RMDs later.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The age of 70½ was the RMD age prior to the SECURE Act of 2019 and is no longer applicable for individuals born after June 30, 1949.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The SECURE 2.0 Act of 2022 increased the RMD age to 75 for individuals born in 1960 or later, effective January 1, 2033. Since the individual was born in 1965, they fall under this new rule and must begin taking RMDs at age 75.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The SECURE 2.0 Act initially increased the RMD age to 73 for those born between 1951 and 1959. This age does not apply to someone born in 1965.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The SECURE Act of 2019 increased the RMD age to 72. This age applies to those who turn 70 1/2 after December 31, 2019, and before January 1, 2023. SECURE 2.0 further increased the RMD age, making 72 incorrect for someone born in 1965.

questions.educational: The SECURE Act and SECURE 2.0 Act significantly altered the landscape of retirement planning, primarily by changing the required minimum distribution (RMD) rules. These changes aim to allow individuals to defer taxes longer, potentially leading to greater retirement savings. Understanding the specific birth year cutoffs is crucial for providing accurate advice on retirement income planning and avoiding penalties for clients.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, carefully note the individual's birth year when determining the RMD age. SECURE Act and SECURE 2.0 changes are frequently tested, so memorize the key birth year cutoffs and corresponding RMD ages.
questions.memoryAid: Remember '60 or later, RMD at 75' to quickly recall the SECURE 2.0 rule for those born in 1960 and beyond.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the RMD age changes introduced by the SECURE Act and SECURE 2.0. The exam exploits the misconception that the RMD age is still 70 1/2 or 72. Test-takers often incorrectly remember the original SECURE Act's change to age 72, overlooking the further change to 73 and then 75 depending on birth year.

questions.question: John, a Vista Healthcare LLC employee, has both a traditional 401(k) and a Roth 401(k). Beginning in 2024, which of the following retirement accounts will have RMDs eliminated under SECURE 2.0?
questions.explanation: Roth 401(k)/403(b) RMD elimination (effective 2024): Previously, Roth 401(k)s had RMDs (unlike Roth IRAs). SECURE 2.0 aligns them. No RMDs for designated Roth accounts. No need to roll Roth 401(k) to Roth IRA to avoid RMDs. Roth IRAs never had RMDs. Inherited Roths still have rules. Traditional accounts still require RMDs at 73/75.
options: All tax-advantaged retirement accounts, including 403(b)s and 457(b)s, are exempt from RMDs for participants under age 75.
Inherited IRAs from non-spouse beneficiaries are subject to a 5-year or 10-year distribution rule, effectively eliminating RMDs after that period.
Designated Roth accounts in employer plans (Roth 401(k), Roth 403(b))
Traditional 401(k) accounts will have RMDs eliminated if the account balance is less than $50,000 at the time the participant reaches age 73.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - RMDs are not eliminated for all retirement accounts for those under 75. The SECURE 2.0 Act only eliminated RMDs for Roth 401(k)s and Roth 403(b)s.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While inherited IRAs have distribution rules, they are not the same as eliminating RMDs. The beneficiary must take distributions according to the applicable rule.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - SECURE 2.0 eliminated RMDs for designated Roth accounts within employer-sponsored retirement plans, such as Roth 401(k)s and Roth 403(b)s, effective in 2024. This change aligns the treatment of Roth 401(k)s with Roth IRAs, which have never been subject to RMDs during the owner's lifetime.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The SECURE 2.0 Act did not eliminate RMDs for traditional 401(k) accounts based on the account balance. RMDs still apply regardless of the balance.

questions.educational: The SECURE 2.0 Act made significant changes to retirement plan rules, including the elimination of RMDs for Roth 401(k)s. This change aims to encourage retirement savings by removing a disincentive to keep assets in Roth accounts within employer plans. Understanding these changes is crucial for advising clients on optimal retirement income strategies and tax planning.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to the effective dates of SECURE Act and SECURE 2.0 provisions, as many changes are phased in over several years. Knowing the specific year a provision takes effect is key to answering questions correctly.
questions.memoryAid: RMDs: Roth 401(k)s are 'Removed' (eliminated) by SECURE 2.0, but Traditional accounts still 'Remain'.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly assume that SECURE 2.0 eliminated RMDs for all Roth accounts or all accounts for those under a certain age. The exam exploits the misconception that all Roth accounts are treated the same for RMD purposes. Test-takers often incorrectly generalize the Roth 401(k) exception to all Roth accounts.

questions.question: Sarah failed to take her full RMD last year. SECURE 2.0 significantly reduced the penalty. SECURE 2.0 reduced the penalty for failure to take a required minimum distribution from:
questions.explanation: RMD penalty reduction (effective 2023): Previous penalty: 50% of shortfall. New: 25% of shortfall. If corrected during IRS correction window (generally within 2 years): reduced to 10%. Example: Miss $20,000 RMD, penalty = $5,000 (25%). Correct timely = $2,000 (10%). Still important to take RMDs, but less draconian penalty for honest mistakes.
options: 50% to 10%, but only for beneficiaries inheriting retirement accounts, not the original account owner.
RMD penalties were eliminated entirely for taxpayers over age 75 who are receiving Social Security benefits.
25% to 0%, but only if the under-distribution was due to reasonable error and is corrected within two years.
50% to 25%, with further reduction to 10% if corrected within the IRS correction window

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The reduction to 10% applies to the original account owner, and there is a correction window for further reduction. The beneficiary rule is incorrect.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - RMD penalties were not eliminated entirely for any group of taxpayers. SECURE 2.0 only reduced the penalty amount.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The initial penalty was reduced to 25%, not from 25%. While a correction window exists, the penalty is not reduced to 0%.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - The SECURE 2.0 Act reduced the penalty for failure to take a required minimum distribution from 50% to 25% of the shortfall. Furthermore, if the error is corrected within the IRS's two-year correction window, the penalty is further reduced to 10% of the shortfall. This tiered approach incentivizes prompt correction of RMD errors.

questions.educational: The Required Minimum Distribution (RMD) rules are designed to ensure that retirement savings are eventually distributed and taxed. The SECURE 2.0 Act's reduction in RMD penalties aims to alleviate the burden on retirees who inadvertently miss RMDs, recognizing that mistakes can happen, especially with increasingly complex retirement planning. This change aligns with the broader goal of encouraging retirement savings and ensuring accessibility to those savings during retirement.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to the specific details of SECURE Act provisions, including effective dates and conditions for penalty reductions. Remember the two-tiered penalty structure for RMDs: 25% initially, reduced to 10% if corrected within the IRS correction window.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '25-10 SECURE' - 25% initial penalty, potentially reduced to 10% under SECURE 2.0.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the penalty reduction for failing to take RMDs under SECURE 2.0. The exam exploits the misconception that the penalty remained at 50% or was completely eliminated. Test-takers often incorrectly remember the original penalty or misinterpret the provisions for further reduction if corrected within a specific timeframe.
